q: What is the difference between `val`, `var`, and `def` in Scala? When should you prefer each?
a: `val` creates an immutable binding evaluated once at definition time â€” the reference cannot be reassigned, supporting referential transparency. `var` creates a mutable binding that can be reassigned, breaking functional guarantees and causing thread-safety issues. `def` defines a method or computed value that is re-evaluated every time it is called with no result caching. Prefer `val` by default; reach for `def` for parameterized computations; treat `var` as a code smell unless performance-critical local mutation is the only option.
fj: I use val for things that don't change and var for things that do â€” that's basically it.
fs: I default to `val` everywhere; it enables safe sharing across threads without synchronization. The moment I see a `var`, I ask whether an accumulator or fold achieves the same result immutably.

followups.q: What is referential transparency and why do Scala devs care about it?
followups.a: An expression is referentially transparent if you can replace it with its computed value anywhere in the program without changing behavior. Pure functions and `val` bindings guarantee this. It makes code easier to reason about, test in isolation, and safely parallelize â€” critical in data pipelines where the same transformation runs on many partitions.

followups.q: Can a `val` hold a mutable object?
followups.a: Yes. `val buf = new StringBuilder` means `buf` cannot be reassigned, but `buf.append("x")` is still legal because the object itself is mutable. Immutability of the binding is not the same as immutability of the object. Prefer immutable collections (`List`, `Map`) over mutable ones even with `val`.

q: What does Scala's `case class` give you automatically, and how does structural equality work?
a: The compiler auto-generates `equals` (field-by-field structural equality), `hashCode`, `toString`, a companion `apply` (so `new` is optional), and `unapply` (for pattern matching). It also provides a `copy` method for non-destructive updates. Two case class instances are `==` if all their fields compare equal, unlike plain classes where `==` delegates to reference identity. Never extend one case class from another â€” the compiler disallows it because auto-generated `equals` would be inconsistent.
fj: Case classes are basically data holders that print nicely.
fs: I use case classes as immutable value objects in ADTs. The auto-generated `unapply` is what powers clean pattern matching, and `copy` is how I do non-destructive updates in pipeline transformations.

lines: case class User(id: Int, name: String)

followups.q: What is `eq` vs `==` in Scala?
followups.a: `==` calls `equals`, which for case classes is structural equality. `eq` is reference equality â€” it checks whether two references point to the same JVM object. `null eq null` is `true`; `"foo" eq "foo"` may or may not be `true` depending on string interning. In practice, always use `==` for value comparison and avoid `eq` unless you explicitly need identity semantics.

followups.q: Why can't case classes extend other case classes?
followups.a: Auto-generated `equals` only compares the fields declared in that class, so a subclass with extra fields would produce false positives. Scala 2 banned case-to-case inheritance outright; Scala 3 maintains the restriction. Use a sealed trait hierarchy or composition instead.

q: Explain sealed traits and exhaustiveness checking. Why does sealing matter for pattern matching?
a: A `sealed trait` (or `sealed abstract class`) restricts all direct subclasses to the same compilation unit. This gives the compiler complete knowledge of possible subtypes, enabling exhaustiveness warnings: if your `match` misses a branch, the compiler warns at compile time instead of the program throwing a `MatchError` at runtime. In Scala 3 `enum` is the preferred syntax for simple sealed ADTs, automatically sealing all cases. Unsealed traits silently lose this safety guarantee â€” a library can add new subtypes and break all existing matches.
fj: I just put a wildcard `case _ =>` at the bottom to avoid warnings.
fs: A `_` wildcard silences the compiler but defeats the entire point of a sealed type â€” you lose compile-time proof of completeness. I keep branches exhaustive and let the compiler catch gaps when I add new variants.

code.label: unsealed = silent runtime crash
lines: case class Circle(r: Double) extends Shape
case class Rect(w: Double, h: Double)
def area(s: Shape): Double = s match
// Rect missing â€” MatchError at runtime

code.label: sealed = compile-time safety
lines: case class Circle(r: Double) extends Shape
case class Rect(w: Double, h: Double)
def area(s: Shape): Double = s match

followups.q: How does Scala 3's `enum` improve on sealed traits for ADTs?
followups.a: Scala 3 `enum` is syntax sugar that automatically seals and creates case objects/classes. It also provides `ordinal`, `values`, and `valueOf` for free, and each case can carry parameters: `enum Expr { case Num(n: Int); case Add(l: Expr, r: Expr) }`. This is more concise than the Scala 2 `sealed trait + case class` boilerplate.

followups.q: What are pattern match guards and what's a gotcha with them?
followups.a: Guards add an `if` predicate to a case arm: `case x if x > 0 => ...`. The gotcha is that a guard makes the branch non-exhaustive â€” even if all cases are covered without guards, adding a guard means "some values of this case go elsewhere". The compiler will issue an exhaustiveness warning if no default catches the unguarded remainder.

q: How do Scala extractors and custom `unapply` work? Walk me through a non-case-class example.
a: `unapply` is the method the pattern-matching compiler calls to destructure a value. Case classes get it for free, but any object can define `unapply(x: T): Option[U]` to become usable as a pattern. Returning `Some(extracted)` signals a match; `None` signals failure. You can also return a `Boolean` for presence-only patterns, or return a tuple via `unapplySeq` for variable-length extraction. Extractors decouple data representation from pattern syntax â€” useful for parsing, validation, and domain-specific patterns.
fj: I just use case classes; I've never written a custom unapply.
fs: I use custom extractors when the match logic can't live in the data class itself â€” e.g., an `Email` extractor that validates format, keeping validation co-located with the pattern syntax rather than scattered across callers.

followups.q: What is `unapplySeq` and when would you use it?
followups.a: `unapplySeq` returns `Option[Seq[T]]` and enables variable-length patterns like `case List(a, b, rest @ _*)`. The standard library's `List` extractor uses `unapplySeq`. You'd write your own for CSV rows, command-line args, or any variable-arity destructuring.

q: Walk me through `Option`, `Either`, and `Try` â€” when do you reach for each?
a: `Option[A]` models optional presence: `Some(value)` or `None`. Use it when absence is routine and expected (e.g., a lookup that may miss). `Either[L, R]` models a two-way result; by convention `Left` carries the error and `Right` the success value. Use it when the error type matters and you want to propagate it (validation, parsing). `Try[A]` is effectively `Either[Throwable, A]` but for exception-throwing code: `Success(value)` or `Failure(exception)`. All three are monadic â€” use `map`, `flatMap`, and `for`-comprehensions to chain operations without null checks or try-catch nesting.
fj: I use Option for nulls and wrap risky calls in try-catch.
fs: My default is `Option` for simple optionality and `Either[AppError, A]` for domain error channels. I reserve `Try` for wrapping Java APIs that throw exceptions, then immediately convert to `Either` at the boundary to keep the rest of the codebase exception-free.

followups.q: What is the difference between `.getOrElse` and `.fold` on an Option?
followups.a: `.getOrElse(default)` returns the wrapped value or a default. `.fold(ifEmpty)(f)` applies function `f` to the value if `Some`, or returns `ifEmpty` if `None` â€” both branches in one call. `fold` is more composable when you want to transform the value and handle absence in one expression without an intermediate `map`.

followups.q: Why is `Option.get` considered a code smell?
followups.a: `.get` throws `NoSuchElementException` on `None`, defeating the entire purpose of `Option` which was to eliminate runtime null-like crashes. It's essentially a controlled `NullPointerException`. Use `getOrElse`, `fold`, `map`, or pattern matching instead. The only acceptable use of `.get` is after an explicit `isDefined` guard, which is still worse than pattern matching for clarity.

q: How does Scala's `for`-comprehension desugar? Show me the `flatMap`/`map`/`withFilter` expansion.
a: A `for`-comprehension is pure syntactic sugar. Each `<-` generator except the last becomes a `flatMap`; the last `<-` with `yield` becomes a `map`. Guards (`if` clauses) desugar to `withFilter`. The result is that any type implementing these methods â€” `Option`, `Either`, `Future`, custom types â€” works seamlessly in `for`. The key interview gotcha: `Either` in Scala 2.12+ is right-biased, so `for` chains go through `Right`; a `Left` short-circuits the whole comprehension, threading the error upward.
fj: for-comprehensions are just loops with yield.
fs: I reach for `for`-comprehensions whenever I'm chaining three or more monadic operations â€” the desugaring to `flatMap`/`map` is mechanical, but the readability gain over nested `flatMap` calls is significant, especially in validation pipelines.

followups.q: Why can't you use `if` guards in a `for`-comprehension over `Either`?
followups.a: `Either` does not implement `withFilter`, so the compiler rejects guard expressions in a `for` over `Either`. Use an explicit `if` inside a `map` or extract the guard into a helper that returns `Either[L, R]`. Scala 3's `cats` and `iron` libraries address this with their own comprehension macros.

followups.q: What happens if you put a `<-` over a `Future` inside a `for`?
followups.a: Each `flatMap` in the desugared chain starts the next `Future` only after the previous completes â€” sequential execution. If you want concurrent execution, start all `Future`s *outside* the `for`-comprehension (so they're eagerly created), then zip them inside the `for`. Starting a `Future` inside a `flatMap` is a common concurrency footgun.

q: Explain Scala's higher-order functions, closures, and currying. What is partial application?
a: A higher-order function takes functions as parameters or returns functions. Closures are function values that capture variables from their enclosing scope. Currying transforms `f(a, b)` into `f(a)(b)` â€” a chain of single-argument functions. Partial application fixes some arguments of a curried function, producing a new function expecting the rest. In Scala you partially apply with `_`: `val double = multiply(2, _)`. A key Spark gotcha: a closure that captures a non-serializable outer class field will throw `NotSerializableException` when shipped to executors.
fj: Currying is when you pass a function to another function.
fs: I use currying to create reusable pipeline stages â€” for example a `def transform(config: Config)(row: Row): Row` lets me pre-bake the config once with `transform(cfg)` and use the resulting function as a DataFrame UDF without re-evaluating config on every row.

followups.q: What is the difference between a method and a function value in Scala?
followups.a: Methods (`def`) belong to an object/class and are not first-class values themselves â€” they can't be passed as arguments directly. Function values (`val f: Int => Int = ...` or a lambda) are `FunctionN` instances and are first-class. Eta expansion converts a method into a function value; Scala 3 does this automatically in most contexts (explicit `_` is no longer required).

followups.q: Why can capturing a `var` in a Spark closure cause silent wrong results?
followups.a: The closure is serialized and shipped to each executor as a snapshot. If the driver mutates the `var` after shipping, executors have stale copies. Similarly, mutations on executors never propagate back to the driver. Use `Accumulator` for distributed counters, or restructure to pure transformations. This is a classic Spark interview trap.

q: Compare `List`, `Vector`, and `LazyList` in Scala's immutable collections. When does each win?
a: `List` is a singly-linked cons list: O(1) head/prepend, O(n) indexed access. Ideal for recursive functional patterns and short lists. `Vector` is a 32-way trie providing effectively O(1) (logâ‚ƒâ‚‚) random access, append, and update â€” the go-to for large immutable sequences with mixed access patterns. `LazyList` (renamed from `Stream` in Scala 2.13) is lazy and memoizes: elements are computed on demand, enabling infinite sequences. The key `LazyList` gotcha: holding a reference to its head prevents GC of evaluated tails â€” a memory leak in long-running pipelines.
fj: I always use List because it's the default.
fs: For any sequence over ~1000 elements where I need random access, I use `Vector`. For truly lazy, potentially infinite pipelines â€” like reading a log feed â€” I use `LazyList` but ensure I don't hold a head reference across the entire traversal.

followups.q: What are collection views and when do they help?
followups.a: `.view` wraps a collection in a lazy view that fuses consecutive `map`/`filter`/`flatMap` operations, avoiding intermediate collections. Unlike `LazyList`, views are not memoized â€” each traversal recomputes. Use views for large collections where you'll apply several transformations and either take a prefix or convert once with `.toList`.

followups.q: What is the difference between `Stream` and `LazyList`?
followups.a: `Stream` (Scala 2) has a strict head â€” the first element is always computed. `LazyList` (Scala 2.13+) is fully lazy: even the head is not computed until forced. This matters for recursive definitions that would loop forever with a strict head. `Stream` is deprecated; always use `LazyList` in modern code.

q: How do Scala traits work as mixins? What is trait linearization and how does it resolve the diamond problem?
a: Traits can contain concrete method implementations and be mixed into classes with `extends`/`with`. When multiple traits define the same method, linearization determines which wins. The algorithm builds a left-to-right, depth-first chain: for `class D extends A with B with C`, the order is `D â†’ C â†’ B â†’ A â†’ ...`, right-to-left-most-specific first, with duplicates removed keeping only the rightmost occurrence. The rightmost (last `with`) trait's implementation wins. `super` in a trait calls the next in line, enabling stackable-trait decorator patterns.
fj: The last trait's method wins when there's a conflict.
fs: Linearization gives a deterministic MRO. I exploit it intentionally with the stackable-traits pattern â€” each trait's `super` call delegates down the chain, composing behaviors like logging, validation, and retry without inheritance hierarchies.

lines: class D extends A with B with C

followups.q: What is the trait initialization order pitfall?
followups.a: If a `val` in a trait is read during the construction of a mixing class before the subclass's `val` is initialized, you get `null` (or 0 for primitives). The fix is to use `lazy val` or `def` in the trait definition so the value is computed after full initialization. This is the classic 'initialization order problem' that often surfaces in test frameworks and DSLs.

q: Explain Scala 2 implicits â€” implicit parameters, implicit conversions, and implicit resolution scope.
a: Implicit parameters are method arguments the compiler fills in automatically from in-scope implicit values. Implicit conversions automatically wrap a value in another type when a method call doesn't type-check directly. The resolution order: (1) local scope and imports, (2) companion objects of the involved types. The critical gotcha: if two equal-priority implicits match, the compiler raises 'ambiguous implicits' â€” a compile error. Implicit conversions are powerful but dangerous and were restricted in Scala 3 (you must now import `scala.language.implicitConversions`).
fj: Implicits are like automatic function parameters the compiler fills in.
fs: I use implicit parameters for type class evidence and execution contexts, but I treat implicit conversions as a last resort â€” they silently change types and make call sites misleading. In Scala 3 I prefer `given`/`using` and explicit `extension` methods instead.

followups.q: What is the implicit search order in Scala 2 and why does companion object placement matter?
followups.a: Search order: (1) local scope / explicit imports, (2) imports in enclosing scopes, (3) companion objects of the type being sought and its supertypes. Libraries (like Cats/Circe) put default instances in companion objects so users don't need imports. If you define a conflicting instance locally, it wins over the companion-object instance due to higher priority.

q: How does Scala 3 replace implicits with `given`/`using`? What changed and why?
a: Scala 3 splits the overloaded `implicit` keyword into intent-specific syntax. `given` declares a contextual instance (replaces `implicit val/def`). `using` marks a parameter as contextual (replaces `implicit` parameter). `summon[T]` replaces `implicitly[T]` to retrieve a `given` instance. Extension methods are now a first-class `extension` block rather than an implicit class. The motivation was that Scala 2 `implicit` served too many roles (conversions, parameters, evidence), making code harder to read and tooling harder to analyze.
fj: given/using is just new syntax for implicits.
fs: The key improvement is intent clarity: `given` is unambiguously an instance for contextual dispatch, `extension` is unambiguously a method on an existing type, and `using` at the call site makes the context visible. I now catch implicit-conversion bugs earlier because the compiler requires an explicit import to use them.

followups.q: What is a context function type in Scala 3?
followups.a: A context function type `A ?=> B` is a function that takes an implicit/given `A` as context. It lets you define DSLs where a given is automatically threaded through without explicit `using` everywhere. For example `type Txn[A] = DbSession ?=> A` lets all functions in a transaction block share the session without passing it explicitly.

followups.q: How do extension methods differ between Scala 2 and Scala 3?
followups.a: Scala 2 uses `implicit class` wrappers that box the receiver and require a wrapper type in scope. Scala 3 `extension` is a first-class construct: `extension (s: String) def shout: String = s.toUpperCase + "!"`. No wrapper allocation, no implicit class clutter, and tooling can find extensions directly. In Scala 3 you can also add multiple methods in one `extension` block.

q: What are type classes in Scala and how do context bounds implement them?
a: A type class is a trait parameterized over a type that defines behavior for that type without inheritance: `trait Show[A] { def show(a: A): String }`. Instances are provided as `given` (Scala 3) or `implicit val` (Scala 2) values. Context bound syntax `[A: Show]` is shorthand for `(using Show[A])`, enabling ad-hoc polymorphism â€” adding behavior to types you don't own. This is how Cats, Circe, and Spark Encoders all work. `summon[Show[A]]` or `implicitly[Show[A]]` retrieves the instance inside the method.
fj: Type classes are like interfaces that you implement for your class.
fs: I use type classes whenever I need to add behavior to third-party types or create principled abstractions like `Encoder`, `Codec`, or `Monoid`. Context bounds keep call sites clean while the compiler proves at compile time that an instance exists â€” a missing instance is a compile error, not a runtime failure.

followups.q: What is coherence and why is it important in type class design?
followups.a: Coherence means there is at most one instance of a type class for a given type in scope. Without coherence, different parts of a program could use different `Ordering[Int]` instances, producing inconsistent sorted results. Scala doesn't enforce global coherence like Haskell, but the convention is one canonical instance per type, placed in the companion object, overridable locally with explicit imports.

q: Explain variance in Scala: covariance (`+A`), contravariance (`-A`), and invariance. Give the intuitive model.
a: Variance describes how subtyping of a generic type relates to subtyping of its type parameter. `List[+A]` is covariant: if `Dog <: Animal` then `List[Dog] <: List[Animal]` â€” read-only producers can be covariant. `Function1[-A, +B]` is contravariant in input: a function that handles `Animal` can stand in wherever a `Dog`-handler is expected. Mutable containers must be invariant: if `Array[Dog] <: Array[Animal]` you could write a `Cat` into an `Array[Dog]`, causing heap pollution. The mnemonic: producers are covariant (+), consumers are contravariant (-), mutable is invariant.
fj: `+A` means you can use a subtype where a supertype is expected.
fs: I always ask: does this type produce values or consume them? Producers covariant, consumers contravariant â€” same as Haskell's functor/contravariant split. Covariant mutable containers are a soundness hole, which is why `Array` and `mutable.Buffer` are invariant.

lines: // (a vet for any animal can treat a dog)

followups.q: Why can't you put a value of type `A` in a covariant `F[+A]` position as a method parameter?
followups.a: If `F[+A]` allowed `def put(a: A)`, and `F[Dog] <: F[Animal]`, then through the subtype reference you could call `put(new Cat)` on what is actually an `F[Dog]`, corrupting it. The compiler enforces that covariant type parameters appear only in output (return type / field) positions, and contravariant parameters only in input positions.

followups.q: What is the relationship between `Nothing` and variance?
followups.a: `Nothing` is a subtype of every type in Scala. Because `List` is covariant, `List[Nothing]` is a subtype of `List[Any]`. This is why `Nil` (typed as `List[Nothing]`) can be assigned to a `List[String]` â€” `Nothing <: String` so `List[Nothing] <: List[String]`. The same logic makes `None` (typed `None.type <: Option[Nothing] <: Option[A]`) assignable to any `Option[A]`.

q: How do Scala `Future`s work, and what is the `ExecutionContext` pitfall with blocking operations?
a: `Future[T]` starts executing immediately when created â€” it is eager, not lazy. It runs on threads from the given `ExecutionContext`. The global pool (`ExecutionContext.global`) is sized for CPU-bound work (number of CPUs). Blocking a thread in this pool (JDBC call, `Thread.sleep`, `Await.result`) starves other futures of threads and can cause a deadlock under load. The fix: use `scala.concurrent.blocking { ... }` which signals the pool to spawn a compensating thread, or use a dedicated blocking `ExecutionContext` backed by a cached thread pool.
fj: I just call Await.result to get the value out of a Future.
fs: I never call `Await.result` in production code â€” that's a thread block that poisons the executor pool. I chain with `flatMap`/`for`, and for truly blocking I/O I use a dedicated thread pool with `blocking { }` to signal the pool manager.

code.label: blocks global thread pool
lines: // JDBC â€” blocks a global thread

followups.q: Why is `Future` eager in Scala and what's the implication for retry logic?
followups.a: `Future { expr }` evaluates `expr` immediately when the line executes, not when the `Future` is composed. Storing a `Future` in a `val` then using it multiple times does NOT re-execute the computation â€” it reuses the single result. To build retry logic you need a `def` that returns a new `Future` each time it's called, not a shared `val`.

followups.q: How would you sequence multiple Futures in parallel vs. sequentially?
followups.a: Create all `Future`s before entering the `for`-comprehension so they start in parallel, then use `for { a <- fa; b <- fb } yield (a, b)`. If you create `fb` inside `flatMap` of `fa`, they're sequential. For a list of futures, `Future.sequence(list)` runs them all and collects results, failing fast on the first failure.

q: What is a by-name parameter (`=> T`) in Scala, and how does it differ from `lazy val`?
a: A by-name parameter `f: => A` wraps the passed expression as a thunk and re-evaluates it every time `f` is referenced inside the method â€” like calling a `def`. A `lazy val` is evaluated at most once: computed on first access, then cached. The combined pattern `def memoize(block: => A): A = { lazy val v = block; v }` gives you a by-name parameter that evaluates exactly once. By-name enables control-flow abstractions like custom `while` loops, retry combinators, and logging DSLs that only evaluate messages when logging is enabled.
fj: By-name is for lazy evaluation â€” same as lazy val.
fs: The key distinction is once vs. many times. `lazy val` = memoized; by-name = re-evaluated on each reference. I use by-name for combinators where I want to defer evaluation conditionally, and `lazy val` when I want initialization deferred but idempotent.

followups.q: Give a real-world use case for a by-name parameter.
followups.a: A logging helper: `def debug(msg: => String): Unit = if (log.isDebug) log.debug(msg)`. The string interpolation `s"expensive $calc"` is only evaluated when debug is actually enabled. Without by-name, the string is built on every call regardless. This pattern is common in Spark's internal logging and in test assertion libraries.

q: What are Scala 3 `opaque type` aliases? How do they improve on Scala 2 value classes?
a: An `opaque type UserId = Int` creates a distinct type at the type level but compiles to plain `Int` at runtime â€” no boxing overhead. Outside the defining object/package, callers cannot see the underlying `Int` representation; only operations explicitly exported work. This provides a zero-cost newtype pattern. Scala 2 `AnyVal` value classes also avoid boxing in simple cases, but they leak the underlying type in a number of situations (generics, arrays, pattern matching) causing unexpected boxing and loss of abstraction.
fj: Opaque types are just type aliases with a new name.
fs: I use `opaque type` for domain IDs, email addresses, and monetary amounts. It catches accidental mix-ups (`UserId` vs `ProductId`) at compile time with zero runtime cost. Scala 2 value classes were fragile â€” you'd box in a `List[UserId]` â€” but opaque types are rock-solid.

lines: // Compile error â€” types are distinct:

followups.q: When would you still use a value class instead of an opaque type?
followups.a: In Scala 2 codebases where opaque types are unavailable, `AnyVal` value classes are the best option. Also when you need to extend a trait (value classes can extend a trait with no abstract methods, enabling polymorphism), which opaque types don't support directly.

q: Explain Scala 3 union types (`A | B`). How do they differ from sealed traits for modeling alternatives?
a: Union types let you write `def accept(x: Int | String): Unit = ...` without any pre-planned hierarchy. The compiler tracks the type and enforces exhaustive pattern matching. Sealed traits require you to create marker traits and extend them before you can have a disjoint sum type. Union types are open â€” types from separate codebases can be unioned without modification â€” while sealed traits are closed. The tradeoff: sealed ADTs carry structured methods and can be extended with behavior; union types are pure type-level constructs best for small, ad-hoc alternatives.
fj: Union types are like Either but cleaner.
fs: I use union types for public API parameters that accept a few unrelated types â€” avoids forcing callers to wrap values. For domain models with rich behavior I still prefer sealed traits/enums because they're self-documenting and can carry methods.

followups.q: What is an intersection type in Scala 3?
followups.a: `A & B` denotes a type that is both `A` and `B` simultaneously. It replaces `with` in type position for compound types: `def merge(x: Comparable & Serializable)`. Intersection types are commutative (`A & B` = `B & A`) unlike `with` in Scala 2 which was order-dependent. They're used for type-safe mixin constraints without creating a new named type.

q: How does significant indentation (optional braces) work in Scala 3, and when should you use it?
a: Scala 3 introduces indentation-sensitive parsing as an option: a colon at the end of certain keywords (`if`, `for`, `match`, `class`, `def`, etc.) opens an indented region instead of requiring curly braces. The rule is that statements in the same indented block must align at the same column. Braces still work and can be freely mixed; the style is chosen per file or block. The official guidance is to use significant indentation for new Scala 3 code, especially for conciseness in DSLs, but the Scala team allows either in a mixed codebase.
fj: You just remove curly braces and use spaces like Python.
fs: The parser resolves indentation via the `<indent>/<outdent>` token model â€” it's not whitespace-sensitive everywhere, only after specific keywords. I enable it in new Scala 3 modules but keep braces in shared codebases with Scala 2 contributors to avoid training friction.

followups.q: What is the `then` keyword in Scala 3?
followups.a: `then` disambiguates `if` expressions without braces: `if cond then expr else expr`. It replaces the need for a brace or newline to separate the condition from the then-branch, making the intent explicit in one-liner form. In Scala 2 you'd write `if (cond) expr else expr` with mandatory parentheses around the condition; Scala 3 drops both.

q: How does Scala 3's `enum` improve algebraic data type (ADT) modeling over Scala 2?
a: Scala 3 `enum` is first-class syntax for sealed ADTs. Simple cases become `case` entries without boilerplate. Parameterized cases work like case classes: `enum Expr { case Num(n: Int); case Add(l: Expr, r: Expr) }`. The compiler auto-generates `ordinal`, `fromOrdinal`, and `values` for simple enums, enabling serialization. Enums can implement traits and have methods. Compared to Scala 2's `sealed trait + case class + companion object` triple, Scala 3 enums reduce the ADT ceremony by roughly 60%.
fj: Scala 3 enums are like Java enums but nicer.
fs: I use `enum` for every domain sum type in Scala 3. The `ordinal`/`values` generation is a gift for JSON codecs â€” Circe's automatic derivation works cleanly with enums. For recursive ADTs like expression trees, enum parameterized cases are as expressive as the Scala 2 approach with far less noise.

lines: def area(s: Shape): Double = s match

q: What is the Dataset vs DataFrame distinction in Spark Scala, and when does compile-time type safety matter?
a: `DataFrame` is `Dataset[Row]` â€” schema is known only at runtime; column name typos and type mismatches surface as runtime exceptions. `Dataset[T]` is typed: `T` is a case class or primitive, and the compiler checks transformations at compile time. Encoders (implicit `Encoder[T]`) bridge JVM objects to Spark's Tungsten binary format. The tradeoff: typed Datasets pay a small serialization overhead and make some optimizations harder for Catalyst; DataFrames are more dynamically flexible. For production pipelines with many transformations, the compile-time safety of `Dataset[T]` pays off significantly in catching bugs before deployment.
fj: Dataset is just DataFrame with generics.
fs: I use `Dataset[T]` at pipeline boundaries where the schema is the contract, and switch to `DataFrame` operations for complex analytics where Catalyst needs freedom to optimize. The encoder is the linchpin â€” if your case class contains a non-Encoder-supported type (like a custom wrapper), Spark will fail at runtime rather than compile time unless you write a custom encoder.

lines: // TypeError at compile if field wrong

followups.q: What is an Encoder in Spark and how is it derived?
followups.a: An `Encoder[T]` describes how to serialize a JVM object to Spark's Tungsten binary row format and back, enabling on-the-fly access to individual fields without full deserialization. For case classes, `Encoders.product[T]` (available implicitly via `spark.implicits._`) derives it via reflection/macro. For primitives, predefined encoders exist. Custom encoders are needed for third-party types like `java.sql.Timestamp` or wrapper types.

followups.q: What performance difference exists between typed Dataset operations and DataFrame operations?
followups.a: DataFrame/SQL operations run entirely through Catalyst optimizer and Tungsten codegen â€” Spark can reorder, push-down, and fuse operations freely. Typed `Dataset` lambdas (`.map`, `.filter` with Scala functions) are opaque to Catalyst: they run as black-box JVM code, losing query planning benefits. For complex analytics, prefer DataFrame column expressions or SQL. Use typed Dataset for row-level domain logic where type safety outweighs query optimization.

q: Why does a Spark closure that captures a non-serializable object field throw `NotSerializableException`?
a: Spark serializes the entire closure (the function and all its captured references) via Java serialization before shipping it to executors. If the closure captures a reference to an outer `class` instance (e.g., a field access like `this.config.value`), Spark tries to serialize the entire enclosing object. If that class is not `Serializable`, Spark throws `NotSerializableException` at job submission time. The fix is to extract the needed values into local `val`s inside the closure so only primitives/strings are captured, not the object reference.
fj: Make the class implement Serializable.
fs: I always extract driver-side values to local vals before the lambda: `val threshold = this.config.threshold; ds.filter(_.value > threshold)`. That captures only the `Int`, not `this`. Implementing `Serializable` on a class with database connections or SparkContext fields causes worse problems â€” those objects must never be shipped to executors.

followups.q: How do you handle driver-side configuration that executors need, such as database credentials?
followups.a: Use broadcast variables for large read-only objects: `val bcCfg = sc.broadcast(config)` then access `bcCfg.value` inside the closure. Broadcast variables are serialized once per executor rather than once per task, and Spark serializes only the broadcast wrapper. For credentials specifically, also consider Spark's secret management or environment variables so secrets never appear in lineage logs.

q: What is the difference between `==` and `eq` in Scala, and when does `==` call `equals` vs. `eq`?
a: `==` in Scala is defined as null-safe `equals` â€” it delegates to the `equals` method and handles `null` without throwing NPE (unlike Java's `==`). For case classes, `equals` is auto-generated as structural equality. `eq` is reference equality, checking JVM object identity. `null == null` is `true` via Scala's null-safe `==`; `null eq null` is also `true`. The common gotcha: comparing two `new String("a")` instances with `==` returns `true` (structural), while `eq` returns `false` (different heap objects).
fj: == is structural equality and eq is reference equality â€” that's all.
fs: The subtle part is that `==` calls `equals` which is overridable. For collection-heavy data work I verify that custom domain types correctly override `equals`/`hashCode` as a pair â€” a broken `hashCode` can make case class instances disappear silently from `Set`s and `Map`s.

q: How does pattern matching work with guards, nested patterns, and tuple destructuring?
a: Pattern matching in Scala is an expression (returns a value). Guards add `if` predicates to a case: `case x if x > 0 =>`. Nested patterns destructure recursively: `case Some(List(h, _*)) =>`. Tuple destructuring uses positional patterns: `case (a, b) =>`. The compiler checks exhaustiveness for sealed types and warns about unreachable cases if a more specific pattern appears after a general one. Unlike Java `switch`, Scala patterns bind variables, destructure, and guard in one unified syntax.
fj: Pattern matching is like a switch statement with extra syntax.
fs: I use nested patterns aggressively in ETL to decode nested JSON structures into domain types in one match expression, letting the compiler prove all cases are handled. The `@` binding operator (`case h @ Head(_) =>`) lets me capture the whole matched structure while still destructuring â€” essential for logging the raw value on error.

lines: def describe(x: Any): String = x match
case n: Int if n>0 => s"pos $n"

followups.q: What does the `@` operator do in a pattern?
followups.a: `case full @ Some(v) =>` binds `full` to the entire matched `Some(v)` and `v` to the inner value. This lets you use both the whole structure and its parts without extracting twice. It's especially useful when you want to log the raw input for debugging while also working with the destructured value.

q: What is the `collect` method on Scala collections and how does it differ from `filter` + `map`?
a: `collect` takes a partial function and applies it only to elements for which it is defined â€” combining filter and map atomically in one pass. It avoids the two-pass cost of `filter` followed by `map` and produces cleaner code. The partial function's `isDefinedAt` performs the filtering, and `apply` does the transformation. A common use is extracting and transforming a specific subtype from a mixed collection: `list.collect { case Right(v) => v }` â€” no explicit `filter(_.isRight).map(_.right.get)` needed.
fj: collect is like filter but with a transformation.
fs: I reach for `collect` whenever I'm filtering on type or shape while simultaneously extracting a field. It reads as a single intent rather than two sequential operations, and on large in-memory collections the single-pass character matters for cache friendliness.

q: Explain Scala's `Try` and how to recover from failures using `recover` and `recoverWith`.
a: `Try[A]` is either `Success(value)` or `Failure(exception)`. `recover` takes a partial function from `Throwable` to `A` and converts matching failures to successes â€” useful for providing default values on specific exceptions. `recoverWith` takes a partial function returning `Try[A]`, enabling fallback computations that may themselves fail. Chaining: `Try(parse(s)).recover { case _: NumberFormatException => 0 }`. For non-exceptional expected failures, convert early to `Either` with `.toEither` for richer error typing.
fj: Just wrap the whole thing in a try-catch.
fs: I use `Try` only at Java API boundaries, then immediately `.toEither.left.map(mapException)` to get a typed error domain. Scattered `Try`/`recover` chains across business logic are a smell â€” they mix exception semantics with functional flow control.

lines: def safeDiv(a: Int, b: Int): Try[Int] =

followups.q: What is the difference between `recover` and `recoverWith`?
followups.a: `recover` maps a `Failure` to a plain value, producing `Success(value)`. `recoverWith` maps a `Failure` to another `Try`, enabling a fallback that may itself fail (e.g., trying an alternative source). The name mirrors `flatMap` vs `map`: `recoverWith` is the flatMap equivalent for the failure channel.

q: What is the difference between `map` and `flatMap` on `Option`, and why does nesting arise without `flatMap`?
a: `map` applies a function `A => B` to the `Some(a)` value, producing `Option[B]`. If the function itself returns an `Option`, you get `Option[Option[B]]` â€” a nested wrapper. `flatMap` applies `A => Option[B]` and flattens one layer, producing `Option[B]` directly. This flattening is what makes monadic chaining work: multiple nullable lookups compose with `flatMap` or `for` without accumulating layers. The same principle applies to `Either`, `Future`, and `List`.
fj: I use map and if I get a double-wrapped result I just call .get.get or flatten after.
fs: Double-wrapping is the signal to switch from `map` to `flatMap`. In pipeline code I watch for `Option[Option[_]]` return types as a code smell â€” it means a `map` was used where `flatMap` was needed, and the outer layer will swallow the inner `None` silently on subsequent operations.

q: What is tail recursion and the `@tailrec` annotation in Scala? Why does the JVM require it explicitly?
a: Tail recursion is a recursive call that is the very last action in a function, allowing the compiler to replace the call with a `GOTO` â€” reusing the current stack frame instead of allocating a new one. Scala's `@tailrec` annotation instructs the compiler to either optimize the call or emit a compile error if the tail-call optimization is impossible (e.g., the recursive call is not in tail position). The JVM has no native tail-call opcode, so Scala performs this transformation at the bytecode level â€” without `@tailrec`, a recursive function on a large input will throw `StackOverflowError`.
fj: I use recursion and trust the stack is big enough.
fs: I annotate every recursive function with `@tailrec` as a correctness assertion â€” if I refactor the recursion out of tail position the code won't compile, rather than crashing at runtime on production data. For mutual recursion, `@tailrec` doesn't apply; I use a trampoline via `scala.util.control.TailCalls`.

code.label: tail-recursive with accumulator
lines: def sum(n: Int, acc: Long = 0): Long =

q: How does Scala's type inference work, and where does it fail requiring explicit annotations?
a: Scala uses bidirectional local type inference (Hindley-Milner extended). It infers types of `val` bindings, lambda parameters when the expected type is known, and most expression types. It fails and requires explicit annotations in: (1) recursive methods â€” the return type of a recursive `def` must be stated; (2) overloaded methods where the argument type must disambiguate; (3) `_` wildcards in complex expressions; (4) implicit/given resolution where the expected type drives which instance is selected. Scala 3 improves inference for polymorphic lambdas and reduces annotation burden vs. Scala 2.
fj: Scala infers almost everything, you rarely need to annotate.
fs: I annotate public API return types explicitly even when inference could handle it â€” it's documentation and prevents accidental type widening from breaking call sites when the implementation changes. I let inference do its job for local vals and lambdas where the context is clear.

q: What is a companion object in Scala, and how does it differ from `static` in Java?
a: A companion object is an `object` with the same name as a class, defined in the same file. It shares access to the class's private members. Methods and vals on the companion are accessed without an instance (`ClassName.method`), similar to Java `static`. The key difference: the companion is a proper object with its own type (`ClassName.type`), can extend traits and be passed as a value, and participates in implicit/given resolution. This makes factory methods, type class instances, and default arguments far more flexible than Java statics.
fj: Companion object is just like static methods in Java.
fs: The companion-as-trait-instance pattern is powerful: placing a type class `given` instance in the companion means it's always in scope without imports. I use companions for `apply` factory methods that enforce invariants (e.g., returning `Option[T]` when construction can fail) â€” much cleaner than constructors that throw.

q: How do Scala's `foldLeft` and `foldRight` differ? What are the stack implications?
a: `foldLeft` processes elements left-to-right, accumulating in tail-recursive fashion â€” it is stack-safe on standard collections. `foldRight` processes right-to-left; on `List` the standard library implementation reverses and uses `foldLeft`, so it is also stack-safe. On `LazyList` and custom structures, `foldRight` may stack-overflow for large inputs because the recursive call is not in tail position. `foldLeft` is generally preferred for deterministic, efficient accumulation. `reduceLeft`/`reduceRight` skip the initial accumulator but throw on empty collections â€” always use `fold` variants for safety.
fj: foldLeft goes left-to-right and foldRight goes right-to-left â€” that's the only difference.
fs: Stack safety is the critical distinction in production. I default to `foldLeft` for all aggregations and only reach for `foldRight` when the right-to-left traversal order is semantically needed (e.g., building a `List` without reversing). For recursive structures like trees I implement explicit trampolined folds.

q: What are Scala's `implicit class` (Scala 2) and `extension` (Scala 3) methods? Show a practical data-engineering example.
a: Both let you add methods to existing types without modifying them. In Scala 2, `implicit class RichDF(df: DataFrame)` wraps the type and defines extension methods â€” every call boxes the `DataFrame` into the wrapper. In Scala 3, `extension (df: DataFrame) def addAuditCols: DataFrame = ...` is zero-overhead and does not create a wrapper type. Extensions are looked up from `given` instances in scope, making them composable with type classes. For data engineering, extension methods on `DataFrame` for audit columns, schema assertions, or pipeline chaining produce dramatically cleaner code.
fj: I'd just create a utility object with static methods.
fs: Utility objects scatter call sites and break fluent chaining. Extension methods let me write `df.validate.dedup.addAuditCols` instead of `addAuditCols(dedup(validate(df)))`. In Scala 3 I group all DataFrame pipeline extensions in one `extension` block, keeping the DSL co-located with its tests.

lines: // Usage â€” reads like a method:

q: Explain the difference between `class`, `abstract class`, `trait`, and `object` in Scala. When do you choose each?
a: `class` is a concrete type that can be instantiated; `abstract class` cannot be instantiated and may have abstract members â€” use when you need a constructor parameter in the base type or Java interop (Java can extend abstract classes but not traits). `trait` is the Scala interface/mixin; supports multiple inheritance and can carry concrete implementations. `object` is a singleton â€” use for companion objects, module-level utilities, and entry points. Scala 3 adds `opaque type`, `enum`, and `given` as first-class alternatives for patterns that previously required creative class/object combinations.
fj: trait is like an interface and abstract class is like an abstract class in Java.
fs: I default to `trait` for shared behavior. I use `abstract class` only when I need constructor parameters in the base (traits can't have class-style constructor params in Scala 2, though Scala 3 `trait` parameters change this) or for Java interop. `object` is my module boundary â€” it's a value, can extend traits, and participates in the type system unlike Java static classes.

q: What is Scala's `Either.sequence` / `traverse` pattern and why is it important for batch validation?
a: Standard library `Either` has no built-in `sequence`. Libraries like Cats provide `Traverse[List].sequence(List[Either[E, A]]): Either[E, List[A]]` which fails fast on the first `Left`. For accumulating multiple errors (fail slow), use `Validated[NonEmptyList[E], A]` from Cats â€” it gathers all failures rather than short-circuiting. In batch validation (e.g., validating 1000 CSV rows) fail-fast is usually wrong â€” you want all errors reported at once. `ValidatedNel` is the canonical Cats pattern for this.
fj: I'd fold over the list checking each Either and stop on the first error.
fs: Fail-fast is the wrong default for user-facing validation â€” users want to see all errors, not fix one and re-submit. I use `Cats ValidatedNel` for multi-field and multi-row validation, accumulating a `NonEmptyList[Error]`, then convert to `Either` only at the service boundary where I need monadic chaining.

q: What is the stackable traits pattern in Scala and how does `super` work in a trait chain?
a: Stackable traits use linearization to compose behaviors: each trait's implementation calls `super.method`, delegating to the next trait in the linearization order. You build a stack by mixing multiple behavior-modifying traits onto a base class. Each layer wraps the previous without knowing the concrete class. This pattern is how Scala achieves aspect-like programming (logging, retry, caching) without frameworks. The key constraint: traits that call `super.method` must `abstract override` the method, signaling that they are meant to be stacked onto a class providing a concrete implementation.
fj: I'd use a decorator pattern with separate wrapper classes.
fs: The stackable pattern beats wrapper classes for behavior composition because the layering is defined at the `class D extends Base with Logging with Retry` use-site, not buried in wrapper constructors. Adding a new behavior is one `with Trait` addition. I use it extensively for test doubles â€” stack a `RecordingTrait` onto the real service to capture calls.

q: How does Spark handle UDFs in Scala, and what are the type-safety and performance pitfalls?
a: A Spark UDF wraps a Scala function: `val upper = udf((s: String) => s.toUpperCase)`. UDFs are black boxes â€” Catalyst cannot optimize through them, they disable predicate pushdown, and serialization/deserialization overhead applies on every row. Type safety is lost: the UDF's return type must be declared explicitly or Spark infers it incorrectly. In Spark 3.x, typed `Dataset` transformations and built-in `functions.*` are always preferable. Use UDFs only for logic that has no built-in equivalent. Prefer `mapPartitions` over per-row UDFs when initialization cost (e.g., model loading) is high.
fj: UDFs are great for custom logic that SQL can't do.
fs: UDFs are a last resort. I scan `org.apache.spark.sql.functions._` first â€” almost all common transforms are there as Catalyst-optimized expressions. When I must write a UDF, I always specify the return type explicitly, add null handling, and benchmark against a `Dataset[T].map` to choose the lesser evil.

followups.q: What is `mapPartitions` and when does it outperform `map` with a UDF?
followups.a: `mapPartitions(iter => ...)` receives the full partition iterator, letting you initialize expensive resources once per partition rather than once per row. Loading an ML model, opening a DB connection, or reading a lookup file inside `mapPartitions` is O(partitions) initialization cost vs O(rows) for per-row `map`/UDF. The tradeoff is that all rows of a partition stay in memory simultaneously, so watch partition size.

q: Explain Scala's `lazy val` thread-safety guarantee and its double-checked locking implementation.
a: A `lazy val` in Scala is thread-safe by default: the JVM-level implementation uses a `volatile` flag and synchronized block to guarantee single initialization under concurrent access. The first thread to evaluate it acquires a monitor lock, computes the value, sets the volatile flag; subsequent threads see the flag and skip evaluation. This is the double-checked locking (DCL) pattern â€” safe on JVM because `volatile` ensures memory visibility. The cost: each access reads a `volatile` field. For hot code paths this overhead can be measurable; use an eager `val` if the object is always needed.
fj: lazy val is thread-safe because Scala makes it synchronized.
fs: The volatile flag read is the per-access cost of `lazy val`. In a tight loop over millions of rows I've seen `lazy val` fields cause measurable slowdowns from repeated volatile reads. For initialization-heavy singletons in Spark transformations I initialize eagerly in the enclosing scope once and use a plain `val`.

q: How do you handle resource management in Scala? What does `scala.util.Using` provide over try-finally?
a: `scala.util.Using` (added in 2.13) implements the loan pattern for any `AutoCloseable`: it opens the resource, passes it to the block, and always closes it â€” returning a `Try[A]` so cleanup errors are not swallowed. `Using.Manager` handles multiple resources in order, closing all even if one close fails, accumulating exceptions via suppression. Compared to manual try-finally, `Using` is composable, avoids exception-swallowing when both the body and close fail, and integrates with `Try` chains. For functional effect systems (ZIO, Cats Effect), use `ZIO.acquireRelease` or `Resource[F, A]` for composable lifecycle management.
fj: I use try-finally to close resources the same as in Java.
fs: Manual try-finally swallows the original exception if `close()` also throws. `Using` surfacing both via suppressed exceptions is the correct behavior. For complex pipelines I prefer Cats Effect `Resource` which composes arbitrary cleanup actions in `flatMap` chains without nesting.

q: What is Scala's `PartialFunction` type and how does it differ from `Function1`?
a: `PartialFunction[A, B]` extends `A => B` but adds `isDefinedAt(a: A): Boolean` â€” it is only defined for a subset of inputs. The compiler builds `PartialFunction` instances from `{ case ... }` literal blocks. Key operations: `orElse` chains two partial functions (try first, fall back), `andThen` composes with a total function, `lift` converts to `A => Option[B]`. The `collect` method on collections uses `isDefinedAt` internally. Never call `.apply` on a `PartialFunction` without checking `isDefinedAt` â€” you'll get a `MatchError`.
fj: A PartialFunction is just a function that doesn't handle all inputs.
fs: I use `PartialFunction` with `orElse` for building extensible command dispatchers and event routers â€” each handler covers its own cases and the chain composes. The `lift` method is indispensable for converting legacy `PartialFunction` APIs to safe `Option`-returning code.

q: Describe how Spark's Catalyst optimizer interacts with Scala Dataset operations. What breaks optimization?
a: Catalyst is Spark's query planner. DataFrame and SQL column expressions are represented as an AST that Catalyst can inspect, rewrite (predicate pushdown, constant folding, projection pruning), and codegen. When you use `Dataset[T].map(f)` with a Scala lambda, the lambda is an opaque JVM function â€” Catalyst cannot see inside it, so it wraps it as `MapPartitions` with no optimization. Mixing typed `Dataset` lambdas with DataFrame column operations in the same query plan forces a serialization boundary (deserialize to JVM objects, apply lambda, re-serialize). Minimize typed Dataset lambda chains for analytics; prefer column expressions.
fj: Catalyst just makes everything faster automatically.
fs: The serialization boundary between typed Dataset and DataFrame operations is the subtlety most engineers miss. In a mixed plan, Spark inserts `DeserializeToObject` and `SerializeFromObject` nodes â€” visible in `df.queryExecution.optimizedPlan`. I use `.toDF()` to cross back to DataFrame mode after typed operations, giving Catalyst a chance to optimize the analytical portion.

followups.q: What is predicate pushdown and how does it interact with Parquet?
followups.a: Predicate pushdown means Catalyst rewrites a filter so it is applied at the data-source level before data enters Spark. For Parquet, row-group statistics (min/max per column) allow Spark to skip entire row groups without reading them. This requires the filter to be expressed as a column expression (`col("year") === 2024`), not a Scala lambda, because Catalyst can serialize column expressions to the Parquet reader but cannot serialize opaque lambdas.

q: How do you write a Scala type class instance for a third-party type you don't control, and what is the orphan instance problem?
a: You define a `given` (Scala 3) or `implicit val` (Scala 2) in a dedicated object or package object for the third-party type: `given Show[JavaLocalDate] with { def show(d: LocalDate) = d.toString }`. The orphan instance problem: if you define this instance in a file that is not the companion of `Show` nor of `LocalDate`, the instance is not automatically in scope â€” users must import it explicitly. Libraries like Cats deliberately place third-party instances in importable modules (e.g., `cats.instances.java.*`) for this reason. Coherence issues arise if two libraries both provide an orphan instance for the same type â€” users must pick one.
fj: I'd extend the interface and add the method I need.
fs: Third-party types can't be extended, and that's the whole point of type classes. I package orphan instances in a dedicated `instances` object, document the import clearly, and treat it as a contract â€” if a downstream library adds a conflicting instance, the user sees an ambiguous implicit error that surfaces the conflict rather than silently picking the wrong behavior.
